message.content: Adversaries may exploit PowerShell profiles to gain persistence and elevate privileges by executing malicious content when PowerShell starts. PowerShell profiles (e.g., profile.ps1) are scripts that customize user environments and can be configured for different host programs or all users on a local computer. By modifying these profiles, adversaries can insert arbitrary commands, functions, or modules that execute each time a PowerShell session is initiated, unless the -NoProfile flag is used. This technique can also lead to privilege escalation if the profile script is executed by a higher-privileged account, such as a domain administrator. Notably, the Turla group has used this method to maintain persistence on infected machines. Mitigation strategies include restricting write access to profile locations, using the -NoProfile flag for critical PowerShell executions, and monitoring profile modifications for unauthorized changes.
